verses.commentary: This is the written account of Adam's family line. The Hebrew word (toledot, תּוֹלְדוֹת) means generations or genealogy. This structures Genesis into sections. When God created mankind, he made them in the likeness of God. The image of God persists after the fall. Humanity retains dignity despite sin.
He created them male and female and blessed them. Echoes Genesis 1:27-28. When they were created, he called them mankind (adam, אָדָם). Both male and female together constitute humanity.

When Adam had lived 130 years, he had a son in his own likeness, in his own image and named him Seth. Just as Adam was made in God's image, Seth is made in Adam's image. The image passes down but now includes fallenness. The genealogy begins.
The numbers may be symbolic or reflect different conditions. The pattern is: age at son's birth, years lived after, other children, total age, then he died.
a drumbeat. Death is universal. The warning of Genesis 2:17 proves true. Mortality reigns.
The genealogy continues through Seth, the line of hope (4:25-26), not through Cain.
Life goes on. Generations multiply.
Death comes for all.
Enosh means mortal or mankind, emphasizing human frailty.
The generations continue steadily.
No one escapes death.
The genealogy marches forward through time.
The pattern is consistent, emphasizing continuity.
eath remains the refrain.
Mahalalel may mean praise of God, a hopeful name.
The generations continue.
he drumbeat of mortality continues.
We are approaching a significant figure.
After he became the father of Enoch, Jared lived 800 years and had other sons and daughters. The pattern holds.
he longest lifespan so far, yet death still comes.
Enoch's story will break the pattern.
Only Enoch and Noah receive this description in Genesis. Faithfulness is possible even in a fallen world.
His lifespan is dramatically shorter than others, but the significance lies not in length but in what follows.
Enoch does not die but is taken by God. Death is not absolute. There is a way beyond mortality. Hope interrupts the drumbeat.
Methuselah will have the longest recorded lifespan in Scripture.
The pattern resumes after Enoch's exception.
Yet death still comes. Methuselah's death likely coincides with the flood, a boundary marker.
t (4:18-24). He belongs to Seth's line.

He named him Noah (Noach, נֹחַ) and said, He will comfort us in the labor and painful toil of our hands caused by the ground the LORD has cursed. Noah's name means rest or comfort. Lamech expresses hope that Noah will bring relief from the curse of Genesis 3:17. This anticipates the flood and new beginning.
The pattern continues.
Death comes even to the father of Noah.
The genealogy reaches Noah, the hinge figure. From him will come a new humanity after the flood. The stage is set for the next act of the drama.
